coredata.dc:title: Dense nano-tips homogenize lithium deposition

coredata.dc:description: Lithium metal batteries (LMBs) offer high energy density but suffer from dendrite growth and interfacial instability, hindering practical application. We present a novel strategy that repurposes the “tip effect” to achieve uniform lithium deposition and suppress dendrite formation. By designing a three-dimensional Cu/Fe3O4 Mott-Schottky heterojunction array with a high-density nano-tip structure, we homogenize the surface charge distribution, preventing current hotspots that induce dendrite growth. The Mott-Schottky heterojunction generates a robust built-in electric field that enriches Li+ concentration at the electrode surface, mitigates Li+ depletion, and homogenizes the electric field distribution. Simultaneously, the ferromagnetic Fe3O4 induces an internal magnetic fields, utilizing the magnetohydrodynamic effect, redirects Li+ trajectories away from surface protrusions, thereby suppressing dendritic nucleation. Experimental and computational analysis confirm that this beneficial tip effect and coupled dual-field mechanism can effectively promote uniform lithium deposition, achieving a plating and stripping Coulombic efficiency of 99.2%. Consequently, the symmetric cell achieves an ultralong cycle life of over 3000 h at 1 mA cm−2 with an ultralow overpotential of 12 mV. When paired with a high-loading LiFePO4 cathode (11.25 mg cm−2), the full cell maintains 95% of its initial capacity after 200 cycles, demonstrating exceptional rate capability and interfacial stability. For high-voltage cathode LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811), Li-Cu/Fe3O4||NCM811 cell achieves a capacity retention rate of 94.8% after 150 cycles at 2 C. This work provides an innovative solution for controlling lithium deposition, offering a promising strategy for high-performance LMBs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium metal batteries (LMBs) offer high energy density but suffer from dendrite growth and interfacial instability, hindering practical application. We present a novel strategy that repurposes the “tip effect” to achieve uniform lithium deposition and suppress dendrite formation. By designing a three-dimensional Cu/Fe3O4 Mott-Schottky heterojunction array with a high-density nano-tip structure, we homogenize the surface charge distribution, preventing current hotspots that induce dendrite growth. The Mott-Schottky heterojunction generates a robust built-in electric field that enriches Li+ concentration at the electrode surface, mitigates Li+ depletion, and homogenizes the electric field distribution. Simultaneously, the ferromagnetic Fe3O4 induces an internal magnetic fields, utilizing the magnetohydrodynamic effect, redirects Li+ trajectories away from surface protrusions, thereby suppressing dendritic nucleation. Experimental and computational analysis confirm that this beneficial tip effect and coupled dual-field mechanism can effectively promote uniform lithium deposition, achieving a plating and stripping Coulombic efficiency of 99.2%. Consequently, the symmetric cell achieves an ultralong cycle life of over 3000 h at 1 mA cm−2 with an ultralow overpotential of 12 mV. When paired with a high-loading LiFePO4 cathode (11.25 mg cm−2), the full cell maintains 95% of its initial capacity after 200 cycles, demonstrating exceptional rate capability and interfacial stability. For high-voltage cathode LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811), Li-Cu/Fe3O4||NCM811 cell achieves a capacity retention rate of 94.8% after 150 cycles at 2 C. This work provides an innovative solution for controlling lithium deposition, offering a promising strategy for high-performance LMBs.

This study designs a 3D Cu/Fe3O4 Mott-Schottky heterojunction with a high-density nano-tip structure, repurposing the “tip effect” to achieve uniform lithium deposition and suppress dendrites. The coupled dual-field mechanism (built-in electric field and internal magnetic field) effectively regulates Li+ distribution. The strategy enables ultralong cycle life (over 3000 h) and demonstrates excellent stability in high-loading LFP and NCM811 full cells.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium metal anodes (LMAs) are considered the “holy grail” of next-generation energy storage systems because of their extremely high theoretical capacity (3860 mAh g−1) and low redox potential of −3.04 V (vs. SHE) [1–5]. However, their practical application has been significantly hindered by uncontrolled lithium dendrite growth and the associated safety hazards [6–8]. According to the Chazalviel space-charge model, when the Li+ concentration at the electrode interface drops to a critical value, a space charge region forms [9–14]. Within this region, a significant enhancement of the electric field drives the preferential deposition of Li+ at protrusions via the “tip effect”, ultimately leading to the formation of dendritic structures [15–19]. Therefore, suppressing or delaying the formation of space-charge regions is key to achieving durable LMBs.

Current research primarily focuses on electrolyte engineering. Strategies such as increasing the bulk Li+ concentration and optimizing electrolyte composition to enhance the Li+ transference number are widely adopted to improve Li+ mass transfer efficiency toward the electrode interface, suppress Li+ depletion at the deposition front, and thereby delay the formation of the space charge region [20–22]. For instance, Yu et al. constructed a localized highly concentrated electrolyte system, which significantly extended the Sand’s time by increasing Li+ activity, achieving a Coulombic efficiency (CE) of 99.97% for lithium deposition/stripping and symmetric cell cycling stability exceeding 1000 h [23]. However, such formulations often face challenges of high viscosity, poor wettability, narrow operating windows, and high cost, which limit their scalability [24–28]. This motivates an alternative strategy: instead of relying solely on complex bulk-electrolyte optimization, can lithium deposition be directly regulated through interfacial and electric-field engineering?

Here, we propose a counterintuitive design concept: rather than eliminating tip structures, we deliberately construct a dense, ordered nano-tip array to transform harmful local field concentration into a beneficially homogenized macroscopic field. We implement this idea using a three-dimensional (3D) vertically aligned Cu/Fe3O4 Mott-Schottky nanoarray that modulates lithium plating via a triple-synergy mechanism. The nanorod array functions as a “meta-homogeneous” scaffold, averaging the surface electric field and Li+ concentration to suppress random nucleation hotspots. The Mott-Schottky heterojunction creates a strong built-in electric field (BIEF), enriching Li+ at the interface and promoting bottom-up filling within the 3D framework. Meanwhile, the intrinsic magnetism of Fe3O4 introduces an internal magnetic field (IMF), steering Li+ flux via magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) effects to dynamically suppress dendrite protrusions [29–32].

Benefiting from this “tip-BIEF-IMF” cooperation, the Li||Cu/Fe3O4 asymmetric cell achieves a 99.2% average CE over 640 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 and 0.5 mAh cm−2. When paired with high-loading LiFePO4 (LFP) cathodes (11.25 mg cm−2, N/P ratio≈3.64), the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP full cell retains 95% capacity after 200 cycles. Moreover, the assembled Li-Cu/Fe3O4|| LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) cell exhibits outstanding cycling stability at a high cut-off voltage of 4.3 V, retaining 94.8% of initial capacity after 150 cycles at a rate of 2 C. This work establishes a new interfacial design paradigm for dendrite-free LMBs based on deliberately constructed tip architectures and cooperative multi-physics fields.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the design principle of this study is to leverage a dense and ordered array of nano-tips to transform the detrimental local tip effect into a beneficial homogenized electric field. The conventional “harmful tip effect” originates from isolated, randomly distributed protrusions. According to electrostatic principles, the extremely small curvature radius (r) of an isolated tip leads to a significant enhancement of the local electric field strength (E) (E∝1/r), resulting in intense local field concentration [33,34]. This field concentration attracts an excessive accumulation of Li+ at the tip apex, accelerating local ion depletion and thereby inducing dendrite growth. Therefore, the ordered array structure plays a crucial role in regulating the localization of the tip effect and thereby achieving directional enrichment of Li+ on the electrode surface (Fig. 1a). In this work, the electric fields between adjacent tips in the vertically aligned array couple and shield each other. This coupling averages out the microscale sharp curvatures at the macroscopic level, creating an equivalent electrical interface with a smooth potential distribution, thereby significantly reducing local field concentration. Simultaneously, the close proximity of the tips causes their individual ionic diffusion layers to overlap, forcing Li+ to exhibit significantly enhanced lateral mass transport along the electrode surface. This effectively mitigates the vertical concentration gradient of ions, delays Sand’s time, and suppresses space-charge region formation [35–42]. This optimized structure provides a key interfacial environment for achieving uniform, dendrite-free lithium deposition.

When Cu and Fe3O4 form a heterojunction with different work functions, due to the Fermi level difference, electrons spontaneously migrate from Cu (higher Fermi level) to Fe3O4 (lower Fermi level) until alignment is reached. This establishes a BIEF at the interface directed from Cu to Fe3O4 (Fig. 1b). This field regulates the lithium deposition process in multiple ways by promoting charge and ion redistribution: Firstly, the BIEF facilitates charge redistribution, making the interfacial charge density more uniform. This helps alleviate local current density concentration, fundamentally suppressing the initial growth of dendrites. Secondly, as an additional driving force, the BIEF facilitates Li+ migration. This aids in reducing concentration polarization, thereby enhancing the battery’s reaction kinetics [43,44]. Concurrently, the ohmic contact formed by the heterostructure (Fig. 1c) significantly reduces interfacial resistance, improves charge transfer efficiency and reaction reversibility, working synergistically with the BIEF to achieve efficient and uniform transport of Li+ driven by the electric field. Furthermore, during the deposition process, the longitudinal electric field drive provided by the BIEF synergizes with the Lorentz force generated by the IMF of Fe3O4. Specifically, the synergy between the BIEF and the IMF deflects the trajectory of Li+ [45], inducing helical motion (Fig. 1d). Particularly as Li+ approaches the electrode surface, the Lorentz force guides ions away from their original path, preventing deposition on protrusions and redirecting them toward flat regions such as the electrode bottom. This promotes a more uniform distribution of Li+ on the electrode surface, mitigates ion aggregation, effectively suppresses lithium dendrite formation, and thus facilitates uniform lithium deposition (Fig. 1e).

Overall, the integration of this dense heterojunction array on the copper foil establishes a new paradigm: the deliberate introduction of ordered tips homogenizes the interfacial environment, and dual-field regulation through physical fields jointly enables uniform distribution and deposition of Li+ across the 3D electrode surface and within the interstitial space, ultimately leading to a macroscopically dendrite-free, uniform lithium-deposition morphology (Fig. 1f).

Fig. 2a and S1 (Supporting Information) depict the synthesis of Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod arrays via a hydrolysis-coupled redox (HCR) process, followed by subsequent thermal reduction. In a standard procedure, Cu(OH)2 nanorod arrays were initially formed on Cu foil (Fig. S2), using a method previously described [46–48]. The arrays subsequently underwent an HCR reaction with Fe2+ in FeSO4 solution, forming an intermediate Cu2O/Fe(OH)3 nanorod. The initial hydrolysis of Fe2+ at the interface between the Cu(OH)2 nanorods and the solution generated Fe(OH)3 deposits and released H+, creating an acidic environment that triggered a redox reaction between Fe2+ and Cu(OH)2. Under these acidic conditions, the enhanced reducing power of Fe2+ facilitated the in-situ reduction and transformation of the Cu(OH)2 nanorod arrays into Cu2O [49], forming a Cu2O/Fe(OH)3 nanorod (Fig. 2 b). Subsequent post-thermal reduction in an Ar/H2 atmosphere converted the intermediate product into a final Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod array. As shown in Fig. 2c, the final Cu/Fe3O4 nanorods retained a morphology similar to the intermediate Cu2O/Fe(OH)3, albeit with slightly shorter and thicker dimensions.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis of scraped samples (Fig. 2d) confirmed the phase evolution: the initial product matched orthorhombic Cu(OH)2 (JCPDS No. 35–0505), while the intermediate product corresponded to cubic Cu2O (JCPDS No. 05–0667). Fe(OH)3, typically amorphous or low-crystallinity under experimental conditions, exhibited no distinct diffraction peaks [50]. After reduction, the Cu2O was transformed into metallic Cu (cubic phase, JCPDS No. 04–0836), and Fe(OH)3 was converted into spinel-structured Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 19–0629). Notably, residual metallic Cu peaks in the XRD patterns (I, II) arose from slight copper powder contamination during scraping. The morphological and structural characteristics of the Cu/Fe3O4 composite were systematically investigated through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. Based on the TEM images in Fig. 2e, the prepared Cu/Fe3O4 composite nanorods exhibit a cylindrical morphology with a diameter of approximately 350 nm. Combined with the cross-sectional morphological analysis in Fig. S3, the Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod array grows vertically on the surface of the copper current collector, forming a covering layer with a total thickness of approximately 10 μm. This array structure not only constructs continuous and unobstructed transport channels for the rapid diffusion of Li+ but also significantly increases the effective lithium storage capacity of the electrode. This helps effectively alleviate volume expansion issues during lithium deposition/stripping processes and enhances the structural stability of the electrode [51]. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging (Fig. 2f) further resolved distinct lattice spacings of 0.207 nm and 0.247 nm, which were indexed to the (111) crystallographic plane of metallic Cu and the (311) plane of spinel Fe3O4, respectively. These crystallographic features were in excellent agreement with the X-ray diffraction data. Elemental mapping (Fig. 2g and S4) demonstrated the homogeneous distribution of Cu, Fe, and O elements. The detailed Energy Disperse Spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum is shown in Fig. S5. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. S6a) identified the presence of Cu, Fe, O, and C elements. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2h) displayed peaks at 710.1 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 722.8 eV (Fe 2p1/2), which are characteristic of Fe3O4. Further deconvolution of the Fe 2p3/2 peak revealed the presence of Fe2+ (709.2 eV) and Fe3+ (711.3 eV), confirming the mixed valence states in Fe3O4. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S6b) showed lattice oxygen (530.1 eV, Fe3O4) and surface-adsorbed oxygen species (531.1 eV, O2). The Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2i) displayed distinct peaks at 932.5 eV (Cu 2p3/2) and 952.4 eV (Cu 2p1/2), which are consistent with metallic Cu (Cu0). This observation was further supported by the Cu LMM Auger peak at 918.6 eV. Additionally, a minor contribution from Cu+ was detected in the LMM spectrum, due to partial oxidation or reduction of the sample. The binder-free, directly grown nanorod array avoids issues related to traditional binders, thereby establishing a robust structural foundation for high-energy-density lithium-ion batteries. The magnetic properties of Cu/Fe3O4 were evaluated using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). As shown in Fig. 2j, Cu/Fe3O4 exhibits ferromagnetic (FM) behavior with an S-shape hysteresis loop and a saturation magnetization of 37.4 emu g−1. This FM characteristic induces an IMF, whose associated lorentz force significantly influences Li+ deposition trajectory. Additionally, Fe3O4 functions not only as a magnetic material but also as an efficient semiconductor. The Mott-Schottky curve indicates that Fe3O4 exhibits n-type semiconductor behavior, characterized by positive slopes (Fig. S7), confirming the formation of a Mott-Schottky heterojunction.

To achieve uniform lithium deposition, excellent lithiophilicity and fast electrochemical reaction kinetics are essential. We used density functional theory (DFT) calculations to compare the lithium adsorption energy of Cu/Fe3O4 with that of bare Cu, thereby verifying the superior lithiophilicity of Cu/Fe3O4. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a–c, Cu/Fe3O4 exhibits a substantially stronger lithium binding capability (−2.43 eV) compared to bare Cu foil (−1.28 eV), providing direct theoretical evidence for its superior lithiophilic nature. Differential charge density analysis (Fig. 3d and e) further reveals significant charge transfer between lithium atoms and Cu/Fe3O4, further indicating strong interfacial interactions. To visually validate the lithiophilicity, contact angle tests of molten lithium on Cu/Fe3O4 were conducted. As depicted in Fig. S8, the contact angle of molten lithium on Cu/Fe3O4 is markedly smaller than that on commercial Cu foil. These experimental results are consistent with the theoretical predictions, demonstrating that Cu/Fe3O4 exhibits superior lithiophilicity compared to conventional Cu substrates. To explore the enhanced kinetics of Cu/Fe3O4, DFT calculations were used to examine the synergistic interactions between Fe3O4 and Cu. The work functions of them were calculated independently (Fig. 3f and g), revealing that Fe3O4 exhibits a higher work function (4.678 eV) compared to Cu (4.581 eV). Consequently, when these two materials are coupled, electrons spontaneously flow from Cu (higher Fermi level) to Fe3O4 (lower Fermi level), generating a BIEF at the heterointerface (Fig. 3h). As shown in Fig. 3i, there is significant charge transfer at the heterojunction interface of Cu/Fe3O4. This accumulation of negative charges on Fe3O4 enhances Li + adsorption, significantly reducing the nucleation barrier for lithium. Additionally, the BIEF promotes charge redistribution, effectively dispersing charge density and avoiding localized high current densities.

To validate the exceptional rate capability and long-term durability of as-prepared Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite anode, comprehensive electrochemical evaluations were conducted using symmetric cells and half-cells (with Cu foil as the counter electrode). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the two asymmetric batteries were tested under the condition of 1 mA cm−2/0.5 mAh cm−2. However, as cycling progressed, the CE of the bare Cu cell sharply declined, primarily due to loose Li deposition and dendritic growth. In contrast, the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode demonstrated superior Li reversibility, maintaining a high average CE of 99.2% over 640 cycles, which is attributed to its optimized Li plating/stripping behavior. The voltage-capacity profiles of the Li||Cu/Fe3O4 cell (Fig. 4b and S9) displayed stable and overlapping curves across cycles, indicating enhanced reproducibility compared to the bare Cu cell. Notably, the Li||Cu/Fe3O4 cell exhibited a lower polarization voltage than the bare Cu counterpart. Even under varying current densities and areal capacities (Figs. S10–12), the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode retained high CE and cycling stability. To evaluate the morphological stability, we performed post-cycling SEM characterization on electrodes subjected to 80 charge-discharge cycles at 2 mA cm−2 with 1 mAh cm−2 capacity (Fig. S13). The control Cu electrode showed significant surface inhomogeneity, characterized by non-uniform lithium plating, incomplete surface coverage, and pronounced dendrite formation. In stark contrast, the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode preserved its nanorod architecture, with dense and uniform Li deposition observed in cross-sectional views, confirming its superior structural stability. Aurbach Coulombic efficiency (ACE) tests under 1 mA cm−2/1 mAh cm−2 conditions (Fig. S14) revealed an ACE of 99.56% for the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode after 40 cycles, significantly higher than the 98.80% ACE of the bare Cu electrode. This underscores the enhanced Li cycling efficiency and reduced Li loss in the Cu/Fe3O4 system. Additionally, the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode exhibited an ultralow nucleation overpotential of 8.3 mV during initial Li plating, far below the 37.1 mV observed for bare Cu (Fig. 4c). This indicates significantly lower Li+ diffusion resistance and more efficient Li deposition kinetics in the Cu/Fe3O4 composite.

To further elucidate the mechanism behind the superior electrochemical performance of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite anode, this study conducted a systematic investigation combining kinetic testing and interfacial chemical analysis. Tafel polarization curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements (Fig. 4d–f, S15, 16) revealed that this electrode exhibits a significantly enhanced exchange current density (1.56 mA cm−2) and consistently low charge transfer resistance, confirming its rapid charge transfer kinetics and uniform, stable lithium deposition behavior [52]. This excellent kinetic performance is closely related to the unique solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) structure formed on the electrode surface. XPS depth profiling results (Fig. S17) show that the SEI formed on the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 electrode exhibits a gradient composition characterized by “organic outer layer and inorganic inner layer”: the surface contains small amounts of organic components, while the inner layer is predominantly composed of inorganic species with high ionic conductivity, such as LiF and stable Li3N, and unstable by-products like Li2CO3 are significantly reduced. This inorganic-dominated, dense SEI layer fundamentally differs from the loose, organics-rich SEI typically formed on conventional lithium anodes [53–56]. A synergistic relationship exists between the kinetic advantages and the interfacial structure: on one hand, the low charge transfer resistance and uniform lithium deposition behavior facilitate the formation of a homogeneous and dense SEI layer; on the other hand, the inorganic-dominated SEI structure with high ionic conductivity further promotes rapid lithium-ion transport, suppresses electrolyte decomposition and lithium dendrite growth, thereby maintaining the kinetic stability of the electrode interface during extended cycling [57,58]. This mechanism of “rapid kinetics–stable interface” provides a comprehensive and coherent explanation for the exceptional cycling performance of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 electrode.

Moreover, the fabricated Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite electrode not only exhibits optimized reaction kinetics but also demonstrates exceptional cycling stability. As shown in Fig. 4g, the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite anode exhibits an ultralow voltage polarization (≈12 mV) and maintains stable performance for over 3000 h at 1 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2, surpassing the performance of most reported current collector modification materials (Fig. S18, Table S1). In contrast, the Li-Cu symmetric cell experiences a rapid increase in voltage polarization after 500 h of cycling. This degradation arises from the formation of “dead lithium” due to the unstable interface between the electrolyte and lithium metal, ultimately leading to cell failure [59]. For bare Li electrodes (Fig. S19a), the voltage polarization fluctuates drastically, exceeding 200 mV within just 360 h, driven by the high reactivity of metallic lithium, sluggish Li+ transport kinetics, and uncontrolled dendritic growth. Repeated Li plating/stripping cycles exacerbate volume expansion and mechanical instability, accelerating cell failure.

When the current density and areal capacity are increased to 2 mA cm−2 (Fig. 4h), both bare Li and Li-Cu electrodes exhibit severe voltage hysteresis. The Li-Cu electrode shows a rapid voltage lag increase to over 1.5 V within 100 h, followed by dendritic penetration of the separator and catastrophic short-circuiting. Similarly, the bare Li electrode displays significant voltage hysteresis after 500 h (Fig. S19b). Remarkably, the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite anode maintains a low voltage polarization (≈20 mV) and stable cycling for over 1200 h even under these demanding conditions. Fig. 4i compares the rate performance of symmetric cells at fixed areal capacity (1 mAh cm−2) under varying current densities. The Li-Cu/Fe3O4 anode consistently exhibits low polarization voltages and stable plating/stripping behavior, outperforming the Li-Cu counterpart. This highlights the composite anode’s outstanding cycling stability, reversibility, and uniform Li deposition with reduced energy barriers. These results collectively indicate that the Cu/Fe3O4 electrode possesses significant advantages in terms of lithium-ion transport kinetics and the stability of the electrode/electrolyte interface, making it an ideal candidate material for high-performance lithium metal batteries.

The COMSOL simulations vividly illustrate how our nano-tip array fundamentally reshapes the deposition landscape. For the lithiophobic Cu foil, incoming Li+ tend to accumulate preferentially on the upper surface (Fig. 5 a and b), leading to dendritic growth during continuous charging and discharging. In stark contrast, our designed ordered nano-tip array pre-defines and evenly distributes countless “hot spots”, which collectively result in a homogenized Li+ flux and current density distribution across the entire electrode surface (Fig. 5c and d). The BIEF and IMF then act synergistically within this pre-homogenized framework to further refine the ion transport, preventing any localized accumulation and ensuring bottom-up filling. Thus, the array itself is the key to transforming a stochastic process into a predictable and uniform one.

Further experimental validation through FESEM analysis of morphology evolution (Fig. 5e) confirms this mechanism. The Cu/Fe3O4 electrode demonstrates sequential lithium deposition: initial preferential deposition on nanosheets, followed by guided growth along nanorods, and complete void filling. At 1 mAh cm−2 capacity, the nanorod morphology remains intact, transitioning to partial array filling at 3 mAh cm−2, ultimately forming dense dendrite-free layers at higher capacities. This contrasts sharply with Cu substrates showing heterogeneous nucleation and dendritic growth (Fig. S20), where random Li seeding leads to loose, porous structures with excessive electrolyte consumption and unstable cycling. In situ optical microscopy (Fig. 5f and S21) under 10 mA cm−2 current density directly visualizes this divergence: Cu/Fe3O4 maintains uniform deposition while bare Cu develops visible dendrites within 15 min, progressively worsening over time. Collectively, the synergistic effects of BIEF from heterojunctions, Fe3O4-derived IMF, and 3D nanorod array architecture enable directional lithium deposition with spatial confinement, achieving dendrite-free anodes through optimized ion flux distribution and current density homogenization. This design strategy effectively utilizes internal array space for subsurface lithium storage, enhancing volumetric energy density while mitigating electrolyte depletion and interfacial instability.

To assess the commercial viability of Cu/Fe3O4, we constructed full-cell configurations pairing Li-Cu/Fe3O4 anodes with industry-standard cathodes (LFP or low-Co NCM811). Electrochemical characterization revealed superior kinetics in the composite anode system. At practical LFP loadings (about 3 mg cm−2), Cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles (Fig. 6 a) of Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cells displayed well-defined redox couples (R1/O1) across a wide scan rate range (0.1–1.0 mV s−1). Notably, these cells exhibited a smaller voltage gap between R1 and O1 and higher peak currents versus conventional Li-Cu||LFP counterparts (Fig. 6b), demonstrating enhanced lithium-ion transfer kinetics and electrochemical reversibility. The CV curves convincingly demonstrated the advantages of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite anode in achieving superior structural stability, optimized polarization, and outstanding electrochemical reactivity [60]. Additionally, the reaction kinetics of the two cells were investigated by measuring the Li+ diffusion coefficient (DLi +) according to the Randles-Sevcik equation (Equation S1) [61]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the peak currents (Ipeak) of O1 and R1 were linearly proportional to the square root of the scan rate (v1/2), indicating that ionic diffusion is the dominant process throughout the entire electrochemical reaction [62–64]. The slope analysis of linear fitting revealed that the DLi + at both O1 and R1 peaks in the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cells were markedly higher than those in the Li-Cu||LFP control group. These findings underscore the predominant role of Li+ diffusion kinetics in governing the electrochemical processes of the battery systems [65]. Meanwhile, the CV curves of initial cycles of the battery measured at 0.5 mV s−1 are presented in Fig. S22. Compared with the control group, the CV curves of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 electrode overlap more, indicating better cycling stability and reversibility. The electrochemical performance test results (Fig. 6d) demonstrate that the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||NCM811 full cell exhibits excellent cycling stability at 0.5 C, with an initial discharge capacity as high as 187 mAh g−1. After 100 cycles, the discharge capacity remains at 178 mAh g−1, corresponding to a capacity retention rate exceeding 95%. In contrast, the Li-Cu||NCM811 full cell exhibits significant rapid capacity decay after only 40 cycles, while the bare lithium anode-based Li||NCM811 full cell shows a lower initial capacity. These electrochemical behaviors are entirely consistent with the trends observed in the voltage–capacity profiles shown in Fig. 6e–S23, and S24. Further high-rate cycling tests (Fig. 6f–S25-S27) reveal that the cell maintains a capacity retention rate of 94.8 % after 150 cycles at 2 C, highlighting its outstanding high-rate stability. Rate capability test results (Fig. S28) indicate that when the current density is restored from 2 C to 0.5 C, the specific capacity of the cell recovers to 184 mAh g−1, which is essentially comparable to its initial capacity at 0.5 C, confirming the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the electrode structure.

Fig. 6g compares the long-term cycling performance of Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP, Li-Cu||LFP and bare Li||LFP—under high cathode loading conditions (LFP loading≈11.25 mg cm−2, N/P ratio≈3.64) at 0.5C. The results show that the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell achieves an initial discharge specific capacity of 158.5 mAh g−1, significantly higher than that of the Li-Cu||LFP cell (146.3 mAh g−1). This performance advantage can be attributed to the more uniform lithium deposition process and the formation of a more stable SEI film induced by the Cu/Fe3O4 heterojunction. Moreover, the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell demonstrates exceptional cycling stability, maintaining a high capacity retention rate of 95% after 200 cycles. In contrast, the bare Li||LFP cell suffers a sharp capacity decay after 70 cycles, with a capacity retention rate of only 15.5% after 200 cycles, while the Li-Cu||LFP cell also exhibits poor cycling stability, retaining only 30.6% of its capacity after 100 cycles. The selected capacity–voltage profiles of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell (Fig. 6h) further confirm its excellent cycling stability and high capacity retention, in stark contrast to the rapid deterioration observed in the voltage–capacity profiles of the Li-Cu||LFP (Fig. S29) and bare Li||LFP cells (Fig. S30). Additionally, voltage polarization analysis (Fig. 6i) indicates that the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell exhibits the smallest charge–discharge voltage polarization after the 100th cycle, which originates from the faster electron transfer efficiency and Li+ transport kinetics in the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 electrode. Notably, these superior performances are achieved under high LFP cathode loading conditions, fully demonstrating the significant role of the Cu/Fe3O4 heterojunction modification in enhancing the practical application potential of lithium metal batteries. Test results under moderate LFP loading conditions (≈4 mg cm−2) (Figs. S31 and S32) similarly indicate that the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell exhibits clear advantages in terms of initial specific capacity, cycling capacity retention, and charge–discharge voltage polarization. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6j, the Li-Cu/Fe3O4||LFP cell demonstrates excellent rate performance, benefiting from the aforementioned enhanced interfacial stability and reaction kinetics. Even at an ultra-high rate of 5 C, the cell delivers a reversible specific capacity of 75 mAh g−1, highlighting its exceptional fast charge–discharge capability. Remarkably, when the rate is restored to 0.5 C, the cell capacity fully recovers to its initial value without significant decay. In stark contrast, the control cells experience a drastic drop in specific capacity to nearly zero when the rate is increased to 5 C. This extreme performance difference strongly confirms the unique advantages of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 composite structure. Meanwhile, the long-term cycling test results at 2 C (Fig. 6k) further validate this conclusion.

In summary, the above results indicate that, owing to the highly reversible and rapid lithium deposition/stripping reactions, the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 anode can significantly enhance the rate capability and long-term cycling stability of full cells, highlighting the substantial application potential of the Li-Cu/Fe3O4 anode in practical lithium metal batteries.

In conclusion, we have introduced a novel approach to prevent dendritic growth in lithium metal anodes. By intentionally engineering an ordered nano-tip array, we are able to homogenize the interfacial electric field and ion flux, effectively mitigating the very factors that typically drive dendrite formation. This was realized through a three-dimensional Cu/Fe3O4 Mott-Schottky heterojunction array, which functions as a collective field homogenizer, while the built-in electric and magnetic fields synergistically guide Li+ influx to ensure dense, bottom-up plating. Both experimental and simulation results confirm that this strategy eliminates random nucleation hotspots. Consequently, the anode exhibits an ultralong cycling lifespan of 3000 h with minimal polarization and excels in full-cell configurations with industrial-grade cathode loadings. This work shifts the focus from merely addressing the symptoms of inhomogeneous deposition to actively designing the electrode interface at the nanoscale to control the deposition process. We believe that the principle of rational tip engineering introduced here will pave the way for the development of stable metal anodes, extending beyond lithium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the 3D Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod arrays
ce:section.ce:para: Typical fabrication of 3D Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod arrays proceeds as follows. First, 35-μm-thick Cu foil is punched into 12-mm-diameter discs. Following our previously reported protocol, each disc is immersed in 50 mL of an aqueous mixture containing 2.5 M NaOH and 0.1 M ammonium persulfate for 10 min to grow Cu(OH)2 nanorod arrays on the Cu substrate. The resulting Cu(OH)2 arrays are then transferred into 50 mL of 5 mM FeSO4 solution and allowed to react for 10 h. Subsequently, the samples are rinsed three times each with de-ionized water and absolute ethanol, followed by drying at 80 °C under vacuum. The dried specimens are placed in a quartz boat; the quartz tube is purged with Ar/H2 for 30 min to remove air. The tube furnace is then heated at 2 °C min−1 to 180 °C and held for 3 h, before further ramping to 350 °C and maintaining that temperature for 2 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, a black film of Cu/Fe3O4 nanorod arrays on the Cu substrate is obtained.

All CR2025 coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box (with water and oxygen content below 0.1 ppm) using Celgard 2400 as the separator. Battery testing was performed at 25 °C using a Land CT2001A battery testing system. Prior to assembling symmetric and full cells, both the working electrode (Cu/Fe3O4) and control (bare Cu foil) were galvanostatically pre-plated with metallic Li at 0.5 mA cm−2 for a uniform, reproducible Li layer. For symmetric cells, the pre-plating capacity was 5 mAh cm−2. For full cells, this capacity was tuned by cathode type: 7 mAh cm−2 for high-mass-loading commercial LFP, and 5 mAh cm−2 for lab-synthesized LFP or NCM811.

The electrolyte formulations were as follows: For half-cell and symmetric cell tests, a 1.0 M lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) solution in a mixed solvent of 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) and 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) (volume ratio 1:1) with 2.0% LiNO3 as an additive was used. For lithium iron phosphate (LFP) full cells, LB008 electrolyte (1 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in EC: DEC with a volume ratio of 1:1) was employed. The electrolyte volume added in all cases was 50 μL.

Voltage conditions: The charging cutoff voltage for half-cells was set to 1 V. For LFP full cells, the charge/discharge voltage range was 2.5–4.2 V (1 C=170 mAh g−1). For NCM811 full cells, the charge/discharge voltage range was 2.8–4.3 V (1 C=210 mAh g−1). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation within a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. Mott–Schottky plots, Tafel curves, and cyclic voltammetry curves were also obtained using the same workstation. The Tafel curves were scanned within a voltage range of −0.2 to 0.2 V, while cyclic voltammetry curves were measured at scan rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 mV s−1.

The lithium ion concentration distribution and current density distribution under two structural systems were simulated using the finite element-based multiphysics coupling analysis software COMSOL. Control group: Cu planar structure with LTFSI electrolyte. Experimental group: Cu/Fe3O4 rod-like structure with rod diameter of 350 nm and LTFSI electrolyte. The electrolyte exhibited electrical conductivity of 1 mS m−1 and relative permittivity of 78. The coupling processes between lithium ion concentration fields and electric fields in both structures were simulated using the Transport of Diluted Species (TDS) module and Electric Currents (EC) module in COMSOL software. Steady-state simulations were performed using a direct strongly coupled solver with convergence factor of 0.001. The simulation results revealed the Li-ion concentration distributions and current density distributions for both control and experimental group structures.

Hongqin Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xinshuo Li: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Min Ling: Supervision. Xuehui Gao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Dian Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhongwei Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhongwei Chen is a professor at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, director of the National Key Laboratory for Energy Catalytic Conversion. His research interest refers to the development of advanced materials for sustainable electrochemical energy conversion and storage systems.

Xuehui Gao received his Ph.D. degree from Zhejiang University. He is currently a Distinguished Professor at Zhejiang Normal University. His research interest focuses on the development of advanced materials and their applications in energy storage devices, electrocatalysis and lithium batteries.

Hongqin Chen received her Bachelor of Science degree from Tianjin University of Technology. She is now a master's student at the College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Zhejiang Normal University. Her research focuses on lithium-metal anode protection and interfacial design.
